new_risk_factors: Promotional dependency: SSS growth attributed to 'Best Deal Ever' promotion raises sustainability questions if promotion windows close
Transaction-count-driven growth (vs. ticket/pricing growth) may signal limited pricing power
positive_catalysts_cited: Higher customer transaction counts driving same-store sales in Q3 2025
'Best Deal Ever' promotion cited as primary driver of SSS improvement
Multiple promotional windows extended benefit across three fiscal quarters of 2025
key_quotes: same store sales in the third quarter of 2025 was primarily due to higher customer transaction counts
driven in part by our 'Best Deal Ever' promotion
Multiple windows of our 'Best Deal Ever' promotion also drove higher customer transaction counts in the three fiscal quarters of 2025
To a lesser extent, the increase in U.S
payload.mda_summary: The delta introduces a transaction-count-driven SSS narrative explicitly tied to a promotional campaign ('Best Deal Ever') across multiple windows in 2025, which is a moderately bullish signal for near-term revenue estimates but introduces promotional dependency risk that analysts may underweight. The removed language is generic boilerplate revenue-definition text with no forward signal content, so its removal is informationally neutral. The key underappreciated nuance is that SSS gains are volume-driven (traffic) rather than ticket-driven, which may compress per-transaction margins and limit estimate revision upside if the promotion is discounting-heavy.
